name: Lemongrass Coconut Water Kefir
subtitle: Southeast Asian-inspired probiotic soda with fragrant lemongrass and coconut sugar
blrNote: Lemongrass grows abundantly in Bengaluru — many home gardens have it, and it's available at KR Market and vegetable vendors for ₹10–20/bunch. Coconut sugar is available at Nature's Basket, Organic stores, and Amazon India (₹200–400/250g). If coconut sugar is unavailable, use jaggery — it provides a similar mineral-rich sweetness. At BLR temps, 24 hours of 2F works well.

IN.where: Homemade from water kefir grains. See water-kefir-basic recipe.

ingredients.name: Fresh lemongrass stalks
substitutions: Lemongrass tea bags (2 bags, contents only)

IN.where: KR Market, vegetable vendors, or grow your own. ₹10–20/bunch. Grows very well in Bengaluru gardens and pots.
Nature's Basket, organic stores, Amazon India. ₹200–400/250g.

equipment.name: Rolling pin or heavy knife
equipment.notes: For bruising lemongrass stalks to release oils.

tldr: Bruise lemongrass stalks, add coconut sugar and lime juice to finished water kefir, seal and ferment 24–48 hours. A fragrant, tropical, SE Asian-inspired probiotic soda.

steps.title: Prepare base water kefir
steps.instruction: Make a batch of plain water kefir following the water-kefir-basic recipe. Strain out the grains. You need about 750ml of finished plain water kefir.
tips: The 1F should be mildly tart and lightly sweet.
Reserve grains in fresh sugar water for your next batch.
steps.checkpoint: 750ml of strained, room-temperature plain water kefir

steps.title: Prepare lemongrass and coconut sugar
steps.instruction: Trim the top and bottom of 2 lemongrass stalks. Bruise them by bending and pressing with a rolling pin or the flat of a heavy knife — you should hear them crack and smell the citrusy oils release. Cut into 3-inch pieces. Add the bruised lemongrass, 2 tablespoons of coconut sugar, and optionally 15ml lime juice to your swing-top bottle.
tips: Bruising is essential — unbruised lemongrass won't release enough flavour during fermentation.
Use the bottom 2/3 of the stalk — the tender, pale part. The green top is too woody.
Coconut sugar dissolves slowly — give it a gentle swirl after adding the kefir.
The lime juice bridges the lemongrass and coconut sugar flavours beautifully.
steps.checkpoint: Bruised lemongrass and coconut sugar in the bottle

steps.title: Second ferment (2F)
steps.instruction: Pour the strained water kefir over the lemongrass and coconut sugar. Seal tightly. Place in a warm spot away from direct sunlight for 24–48 hours. Burp every 12 hours. The coconut sugar provides good carbonation fuel.
tips: The drink will take on a light amber colour from the coconut sugar.
Lemongrass aroma intensifies over time — 24 hours is subtle, 48 is more pronounced.
At BLR temps, 24 hours usually gives good carbonation with coconut sugar.
The aroma when you open the bottle will transport you to a Thai kitchen.
steps.checkpoint: Bottle pressurized, light amber colour, lemongrass aroma

steps.title: Strain and serve
steps.instruction: Open carefully. Strain through a mesh strainer to remove lemongrass pieces. Serve over ice. This drink is subtle, elegant, and deeply fragrant. Refrigerate for up to 5 days.
tips: Serve with Thai or Southeast Asian food — it's a perfect pairing.
A fresh lemongrass stalk in the glass makes a beautiful, aromatic stirrer.
Add a splash of coconut milk for a creamy tropical twist.
Excellent base for mocktails — add muddled cucumber for a spa-like drink.
steps.checkpoint: Amber, fragrant, citrusy, gently fizzy probiotic soda

culturalContext.story: Lemongrass is the backbone of Southeast Asian cuisine — from Thai tom yum to Vietnamese pho to Indonesian rendang. Coconut sugar comes from the sap of coconut palm flowers, a traditional sweetener across SE Asia and coastal India. Combining both in a water kefir creates a drink that captures the essence of Southeast Asian flavour — citrusy, sweet, and deeply tropical.
culturalContext.historicalNote: Lemongrass (Cymbopogon citratus) is native to South and Southeast Asia and has been used in Ayurvedic and traditional medicine for centuries. Coconut palm sugar has been produced in coastal tropical regions for millennia — it's one of the oldest sweeteners in the world.
culturalContext.significance: This recipe bridges South and Southeast Asian flavour traditions with water kefir fermentation. Lemongrass grows abundantly in Bengaluru gardens, making this a hyper-local recipe despite its Thai inspiration.
relatedTraditions: Palm wine / Toddy (fermented palm sap, tropical regions)
Nata de coco (fermented coconut water, Philippines)
culturalContext.funFact: Lemongrass contains citral, the same aromatic compound found in lemon peel. But lemongrass is technically a grass (Poaceae family), not a citrus — it's more closely related to bamboo and sugarcane than to lemons.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Bruise the lemongrass properly
thingsToAccountFor.description: Unbruised lemongrass stalks barely release any flavour during a cold fermentation. You must physically crack them open — bend, press, and bruise until you can smell the oils. Cut into shorter pieces for more surface area.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Coconut sugar dissolves slowly
thingsToAccountFor.description: Coconut sugar doesn't dissolve as easily as white sugar in cold liquid. Give the bottle a few gentle swirls after adding the kefir to help it dissolve. Undissolved coconut sugar can settle at the bottom.

dehydratorIntegration.method: Not recommended for flavoured water kefir.
tips: Freeze into ice cubes for adding to Thai iced tea or coconut drinks

variations.name: Lemongrass Ginger Kefir
variations.description: Add 10g fresh ginger along with the lemongrass. Tom yum-inspired, warm and citrusy.

variations.name: Lemongrass Pandan Kefir
variations.description: Add 1 pandan leaf along with the lemongrass. SE Asian flavour bomb — citrus meets vanilla.
